verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Chapter XIV.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Argument.—The Author Prosecutes the Same Argument.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: And yet the heretic still shrinks from urging that Christ is God, whom he perceives to be proved God by so many words as well as facts.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If Christ is only man, how, when He came into this world, did He come unto His own, since a man could have made no world?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If Christ was only man, how is the world said to have been made by Him, when the world was not by man, but man was ordained after the world?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If Christ was only man, how was it that Christ was not only of the seed of David; but He was the Word made flesh and dwelt among us?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: For although the Protoplast was not born of seed, yet neither was the Protoplast formed of the conjunction of the Word and the flesh.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: For He is not the Word made flesh, nor dwelt in us.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If Christ was only man, how does He “who cometh from heaven testify what He hath seen and heard,” when it is plain that man cannot come from heaven, because he cannot be born there?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If Christ be only man, how are “visible things and invisible, thrones, powers, and dominions,” said to be created by Him and in Him; when the heavenly powers could not have been made by man, since they must needs have been prior to man?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If Christ is only man, how is He present wherever He is called upon; when it is not the nature of man, but of God, that it can be present in every place?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If Christ is only man, why is a man invoked in prayers as a Mediator, when the invocation of a man to afford salvation is condemned as ineffectual?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If Christ is only man, why is hope rested upon Him, when hope in man is declared to be accursed?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If Christ is only man, why may not Christ be denied without destruction of the soul, when it is said that a sin committed against man may be forgiven?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If Christ is only man, how comes John the Baptist to testify and say, “He who cometh after me has become before me, because He was prior to me;” when, if Christ were only man, being born after John, He could not be before John, unless because He preceded him, in that He is God?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If Christ is only man, how is it that “what things the Father doeth, these also doeth the Son likewise,” when man cannot do works like to the heavenly operations of God?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If Christ is only man, how is it that “even as the Father hath life in Himself, so hath He given to the Son to have life in Himself,” when man cannot have life in him after the example of God the Father, because he is not glorious in eternity, but made with the materials of mortality?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If Christ is only man, how does He say, “I am the bread of eternal life which came down from heaven,” when man can neither be the bread of life, he himself being mortal, nor could he have come down from heaven, since no perishable material is established in heaven?

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If Christ is only man, how does He say that “no man hath seen God at any time, save He which is of God; He hath seen God?”

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Because if Christ is only man, He could not see God, because no man has seen God; but if, being of God, He has seen God, He wishes it to be understood that He is more than man, in that He has seen God.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: If Christ is only man, why does He say, “What if ye shall see the Son of man ascending thither where He was before?”

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: But He ascended into heaven, therefore He was there, in that He returned thither where He was before.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: But if He was sent from heaven by the Father, He certainly is not man only; for man, as we have said, could not come from heaven.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: Therefore as man He was not there before, but ascended thither where He was not.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: But the Word of God descended which was there,—the Word of God, I say, and God by whom all things were made, and without whom nothing was made.

verses.book: A Treatise of Novatian Concerning the Trinity.
verses.text: It was not therefore man that thus came thence from heaven, but the Word of God; that is, God descended thence.
